card.intuition: After every access, rotate the accessed node up to the root using zig, zig-zig, or zig-zag rotations. This gives amortized O(log n) operations and the 'working set' property: recently accessed nodes stay near the root.
card.analogy: Like a well-organised filing cabinet that reorganises itself — whenever you pull a file, it goes straight to the front. Frequently accessed files stay near the front; a sequence of accesses to the same file is O(1) amortised per access.
card.time_complexity: O(log n) amortised per operation; O(n) worst case for a single operation
when_to_use: Cache-friendly ordered sets where recently accessed items are accessed again soon
Implementing link-cut trees (which use splay trees internally)
When average-case performance matters more than worst-case guarantees
gotchas: Splay must happen on every access — skipping it breaks the amortised bound
Zig-zig (same-side grandchild): rotate parent first, then node. Zig-zag (opposite side): rotate node twice
Zig (node's parent is root): single rotation — node becomes root

exercise.prompt: Implement right-rotation and left-rotation, the building blocks of the splay operation.
exercise.starter_code:
class Node:
    def __init__(self, key):
        self.key   = key
        self.left  = None
        self.right = None
        self.parent = None

def set_child(parent, is_right, child):
    if parent:
        if is_right: parent.right = child
        else:        parent.left  = child
    if child: child.parent = parent

def rotate_right(x):
    """
    Rotate x up over its left child y:
        x           y
       / \    ->   / \
      y   C       A   x
     / \             / \
    A   B           B   C
    """
    y = x.left
    p = x.parent
    is_right = p and p.right is x
    set_child(x, False, y.right)   # x.left  = B
    set_child(y, True,  x)         # y.right  = x
    set_child(p, is_right, ___________)  # attach y to x's old parent
    return y  # new subtree root

def rotate_left(x):
    y = x.right
    p = x.parent
    is_right = p and p.right is x
    set_child(x, True,  y.left)    # x.right = B
    set_child(y, False, x)         # y.left   = x
    set_child(p, is_right, ___________)  # attach y to x's old parent
    return y
exercise.solution:
set_child(p, is_right, y)
set_child(p, is_right, y)
hints: After rotating, y takes x's old position — call set_child with the same parent and same is_right flag, but pass y instead of x
exercise.test_code:
# Build:  2
#        / \
#       1   3
root = Node(2)
root.left  = Node(1); root.left.parent  = root
root.right = Node(3); root.right.parent = root

# rotate_right(root) should give 1 as new root with 2 as right child
new_root = rotate_right(root)
assert new_root.key == 1
assert new_root.right.key == 2
assert new_root.right.right.key == 3
assert new_root.parent is None
assert new_root.right.parent is new_root

# rotate_left restores
new_root2 = rotate_left(new_root)
assert new_root2.key == 2
assert new_root2.left.key == 1
assert new_root2.right.key == 3
print('PASS')
